mapping.b_element: number of particles N in the interacting system
singular pairwise interaction kernel coupling the particles

mapping.a_element: asymptotic irrelevance of priorities (TTC becomes equivalent to RSD in the large market)
mapping.b_element: propagation of chaos (k-particle law becomes the interaction-free product mean-field law as N grows)

mapping.a_element: priority-blind RSD benchmark that TTC converges to
mapping.b_element: interaction-decoupled mean-field limit law that the particle system converges to

brief_justification: Both are large-system limits in which a microscopic coupling (agent priorities in A, the singular interaction kernel in B) becomes asymptotically negligible so the system approaches a coupling-free reference (RSD in A, the product mean-field law in B). But the machinery differs decisively: A is a combinatorial matching-mechanism equivalence established by comparing justified-envy statistics of random assignments, while B is a stochastic interacting-particle system converging to a nonlinear McKean-Vlasov law via relative-entropy and Krylov-Khasminskii estimates with a Berry-Esseen CLT; there is no shared governing equation or transferable technique, only the generic 'interaction washes out as the system grows' theme.
